node.title: Is this a common problem? Facing inexperienced interviewers

topic.title: Is this a common problem? Facing inexperienced interviewers

post.content:
So, I have been giving sde2 level interviews for the past month, I gave around 10, and secured 2 offers. 

I have a PR in Europe, both those offers are in Europe, besides these, I have been interviewing in India. 

A common problem I found, more often than not, I am being interviewed by engineers who just became SDE2 within the last 6 months. While, the ones in Europe, I was ALWAYS interviewed by a more senior engineer. 

What difference does that make? 

In our regular jobs, we don't do as much high level design, until we study about it. 
So, explaining that level of system design to a newly promoted SDE2 has been a struggle for me. 

My in-depth conversations make sense, and more senior engineers, understand and help me in guiding the interview further. 

Let me give an example. 

I was interviewing for one the biggest food delivery apps a few weeks ago. The guy kept pushing down for low level, even though it was a high level design round. 

He gave me a standard scheduler to design. I asked him the TPS. he told me 50k. Naturally, I went with a queue based approach, where I had two options narrowed down. Kafka or AWS SQS. 

I went with AWS, for three reasons:
1. I wanted a fail over mechanism, which SQS has with DLQ
2. SQS provides an invisible mode, with a TTL 
3. I worked at AWS previously, so I was better well versed with it

The interviewer had no clue about SQS, and hence ridiculed the solution in the most funny way, he mentioned, we don't need this complex solution, we can just implement the retry on the application layer. 

I knew about his inexperience, when I added an API Gateway, and he said, oh we do not need it, because it is all internal, and internal services do not need authentication to communicate with each other, to which I asked him, then how would you know it is internal and protect againt malicious users, he had no answer. 

But anyway, the interview ultimately, focused on DSA (LC medium and hard) even though the HR had mentioned, it would be System Design heavy.

Ofcourse, I did not get the offer. 

The feedback I got: I design too complex systems, and I have deep knowledge of only AWS technologies. 


Is this happening with only me?

"internal services do not need authentication to communicate with each other" - typical all codeforces, no practical design line of thinking. for an interviewer, I feel one should pay attention and learn new ideas from a candidate if they aren\'t aware of something. Such a rigid attitude is not going to get them far. \n"deep knowledge of only AWS technologies" - that means you have a deep knowledge of good production grade systems. Picking up something different would be a breeze. Isn\'t checking the candidates understanding and design principles the whole point of the interview, which you just proved?

It\'s a common problem these days. HR\'s are not getting accurate panels for particular rounds. Even I was asked to design Uber by the only SDE2 of the company where I was interviewing for(was a startup of course!). Not only for design rounds, even in case of DSA rounds, less experienced interviewers have caused a lot of headache for me since they lack the experience of how to drive an interview.
How does one get a PR in Europe?

Despite being Anonymous, you choose to not mention the company name - shows how professional you are along with all that knowledge. Surely it\'s that company\'s loss to let go of a candidate like you..!

This is something which companies should actively consider. You cannot prepare interviewers for system design interviews. There is no single solution. System Design interviews should strictly be restricted for senior interviewers. SDE2s can effectively interview for Coding rounds but not system design.

This happens with me also, when same level guy ask me about how we can decode the customer details while saving the customer data, I told him my approach and he is not understand even little bit and told me its wrong approach. and in phonepe interview also the same level guy asks me about retry pattern I explained him what I know and how we can implement it, but he is looking different answers for me.\n I think this is common now companies don\'t have so much resource to waste their time in interview and its very common now according to my experience.
